topic: HyperVelocity Engineering VS Code extension - installation, agents, and AI-native data science workflows
hook: Ever wondered how to supercharge your data science workflow with AI agents directly in VS Code? Today, I'll walk you through installing the HyperVelocity Engineering extension and using its AI-native agents to load, explore, and model the Titanic dataset — without writing any code yourself.

sections.title: Install HyperVelocity Engineering Extension
sections.narration: Let's start by installing the HyperVelocity Engineering, or HVE, extension in VS Code. Click the Extensions icon on the left sidebar — it looks like four squares. In the search bar that pops up, type 'HyperVelocity Engineering'. When you see it in the list, click the blue Install button. That’s it — no need for any API keys or credentials. This extension is ready to go out of the box.
key_points: Open Extensions sidebar in VS Code

shots.visual: VS Code Extensions sidebar with search bar reading 'HyperVelocity Engineering'
shots.action: User types into the Extensions search bar; results appear.

shots.visual: VS Code Extensions list showing HyperVelocity Engineering extension with 'Install' button
shots.action: User clicks 'Install' next to extension name.

shots.visual: VS Code showing HVE extension as installed in side panel
shots.action: Extension is marked as installed and ready to use.

sections.title: Meet the Agents: Open the Copilot Chat Panel
sections.narration: Now, let’s explore the agents HVE adds to VS Code. Click the Copilot Chat icon — it looks like a speech bubble in the sidebar. A chat panel will open. In the agent picker at the top, you’ll notice several agent options, including @task-researcher, @task-planner, and @task-implementer. These are your AI partners for end-to-end data science tasks. We’ll use them step by step to work with the Titanic data.
key_points: Open Copilot Chat panel in VS Code

shots.visual: VS Code sidebar with Copilot Chat icon highlighted
shots.action: User clicks on Copilot Chat icon.

shots.visual: VS Code Copilot Chat panel with agent picker showing @task-researcher, @task-planner, @task-implementer
shots.action: Hover over agent picker to show all available agents.

sections.title: Step 1: Research with @task-researcher
sections.narration: First, let’s research the Titanic dataset. In the Copilot Chat panel, select @task-researcher from the agent picker. Type, 'Explore titanic.csv and summarize the data'. Hit Enter. The agent will create a research markdown file. To see the output, look in the explorer — you’ll find a new '.copilot-tracking/research' folder with a markdown file inside. Open it to review the automated analysis.
key_points: Ask to explore and summarize titanic.csv
Review research markdown file in explorer

shots.visual: VS Code Copilot Chat panel with @task-researcher selected
VS Code Copilot Chat with the prompt 'Explore titanic.csv and summarize the data'
shots.action: User types prompt and hits Enter.
User opens the markdown file, highlighting summary and EDA output.

sections.title: Step 2: Planning with @task-planner
sections.narration: Next, let's plan the actual analysis. In the Copilot Chat panel, switch to @task-planner. Type, 'Plan a Python script to load, explore, and predict survival on titanic.csv'. The planner agent will create a plan file in the '.copilot-tracking/plans' folder. Check the explorer for this new plan. Open it to read through the detailed steps the agent has devised for our data science workflow.
key_points: Request a plan for loading and modeling Titanic data

shots.visual: VS Code Copilot Chat panel with @task-planner selected
shots.action: User selects @task-planner agent in the chat.

shots.visual: VS Code Copilot Chat with prompt 'Plan a Python script to load, explore, and predict survival on titanic.csv'
shots.action: User types prompt and submits it.
User opens plan file, highlights step-by-step breakdown.

sections.title: Step 3: Implement with @task-implementer
sections.narration: It's time to bring it all together! Select @task-implementer in the Copilot Chat agent picker. Prompt it: 'Implement the plan for titanic.csv analysis in Python.' The agent generates the complete script — no copy-pasting required. Check the '.copilot-tracking/details' folder for the implementation details file. Open the file to see the ready-to-run Python code. Double-click the script to open it in the editor, then click the 'Run Python File' button at the top right of the editor. The terminal will display the output — likely some basic EDA and survival predictions using the Titanic data. This all happens automatically thanks to HVE's agent workflow.
key_points: Prompt to implement the Titanic analysis in Python
Review generated script, run and check results

shots.visual: VS Code Copilot Chat panel with @task-implementer selected
VS Code Copilot Chat with 'Implement the plan for titanic.csv analysis in Python' prompt
VS Code Explorer showing .copilot-tracking/details/{date}/titanic-details.md and script file open
shots.action: User opens details file, reviews code; double-clicks script to open in editor.

shots.visual: VS Code editor with titanic.py open and 'Run Python File' button highlighted; terminal panel showing script output
shots.action: User clicks 'Run Python File', output appears in terminal (summary statistics, predictions).

recap: We installed HVE Core, explored its agents, and walked step by step through researching, planning, and implementing a data science workflow on the Titanic dataset — all seamlessly inside VS Code using AI agents.
cta: Try out the HyperVelocity Engineering extension in your own projects, and experiment with the other agents like @RPI-agent to speed up more complex workflows. Happy experimenting!
